projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

insights.summary: The consistent 8–9 device presence count paired with zero camera motion over a four-hour evening window is highly atypical for a household with two active dogs and four members, strongly suggesting either neighbor signal bleed, devices confined to a camera-blind zone, or a potential camera malfunction. Verifying camera functionality and presence detection accuracy is recommended to rule out false positives or unreported activity.

The consistent 8-10 device count paired with zero camera motion for four hours indicates the family is gathered in a single uncovered room (likely a media or gaming space) rather than a sensor failure, as network connectivity remains stable.

The sustained 8-10 device count paired with zero camera motion over 90+ minutes indicates the household is occupied but engaged in a prolonged, low-movement activity (e.g., movie marathon or gaming), suggesting a likely camera blind spot in the main gathering area rather than a security concern.

The steady increase in connected devices (9 to 11) while cameras remain completely inactive suggests a quiet arrival or a new device connecting in a camera-blind area, likely indicating an unlogged guest or a family member returning home without triggering motion alerts.

The sustained connection of 8-10 devices over four hours alongside zero camera motion—highly unusual for a weekday with two energetic dogs and two children present—strongly indicates the security cameras may be offline, malfunctioning, or disconnected from the network. This matters because it creates a blind spot in home monitoring while the house appears occupied.

The consistent presence of ~10 connected devices alongside zero camera motion on a Tuesday morning suggests the household may still be asleep or inactive, which is atypical for school and work days, or the cameras have blind spots; worth verifying if anyone needs to leave for school or work.

The rapid device count oscillation (10–13) with zero camera motion indicates Wi-Fi roaming or network handoffs rather than physical presence changes, which is normal for early morning device synchronization and aligns with a typical Tuesday wake-up routine.

The rapid oscillation of connected devices (10–13) alongside zero camera motion during early morning hours points to background network or IoT device synchronization rather than human presence. This pattern is typically benign but should be monitored if it persists, as it could indicate router instability or mask actual occupancy.

The continued high and rapidly fluctuating device count (11-13) at 4:35 AM, combined with zero camera activity, confirms this is not the household awake but rather external network interference or a smart device glitch. A quick network diagnostic is recommended to rule out connectivity issues or unauthorized access.

The presence sensor reports 11-13 active mobile devices between 23:59 and 02:30, which is unusually high for a four-person household at this hour, and the rapid fluctuations combined with zero camera activity suggest possible late-night device usage or Wi-Fi scanning interference. This warrants a low-priority check to ensure the kids are asleep and rule out unexpected guests.
